original_abstract: In both developing and developed nations, the pursuit of economic growth must increasingly be reconciled with the imperatives of sustainable development. Since the Industrial Revolution, rapid global transformation has often come at a significant environmental cost, characterized by the depletion of natural resources and a heavy reliance on fossil fuels. Despite this industrial progress, the resulting prosperity has not been equitably distributed, and traditional economic models have struggled to generate sufficient employment to match the growing labor supply. This study aims to investigate the impact of "green jobs" on the contemporary labor market. Emerging as a critical sector since the 1980s, these roles offer a dual solution: providing a roadmap for environmental sustainability while creating robust employment opportunities to meet the needs of an expanding workforce.
humanized_abstract: Achieving future economic growth will require more attention to the requirements of sustainable development, a goal which is increasingly relevant to both developing and developed countries. We have been living in an age of rapid global change since the Industrial Revolution, which has delivered sustainable industrial development and high living standards on the basis of finite resources and an unsustainable pattern of consumption and environment degradation. Meanwhile, the fruits of industrialisation have not been shared equally, and a paradigm change has been needed in order to ensure decent employment opportunities in line with the growing number of new workers, as previous models of economic growth have been unable to sufficiently create employment. This study aims to assess the relation of green jobs to the labour market. Green jobs have evolved over the last three decades into an emerging and important field of activity which supports sustainable development through the environment, while at the same time opens the doors to stable employment opportunities.
